reasoning: Peer tiles (LEFT) establish a dark dungeon pixel-art vocabulary: cool grey/black stone, teal and red accents, high-contrast hard-stepped shading, dense feature count, strong hand-pixeled charm. Candidate (RIGHT) delivers a sandy beige outdoor wall with warm tan tones, scattered pink accent dots, smoother gradient transitions, and significantly fewer features per tile cell. (1) Palette overlap: near-zero — peers are cool grey/teal/red/black; candidate is beige/tan/pink/blue-grey. (2) Pixel density: candidate is noticeably sparser; peer tiles pack dungeon props, rubble, and texture within each cell. (3) Shading: peers use hard-stepped highlights/shadows; candidate has softer, more gradient-like transitions suggesting different source workflow. (4) Warmth/saturation: peers cool and desaturated; candidate is warm and slightly washed. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: peers show deliberate pixel placement; candidate appears closer to upscaled or procedural source. (6) Photo-realism contamination: moderate — the sandy texture reads as photo-derived or AI-generated flat noise rather than intentional pixel-art mark-making. (7) Overall feel: candidate looks like a different game entirely — sandy ruins aesthetic vs the dark dungeon language of the peer set. Even granting that 'outdoor wall' is a different surface type, the style, palette, and rendering pipeline clearly diverge from the established Echoes visual identity.
